topics.title: Graphs
topics.story: A city road map is a graph — intersections are nodes, roads are edges. Facebook's friend network is a graph. The internet is a graph. Graphs are everywhere because they model relationships. Mastering BFS, DFS, Union-Find, and Dijkstra on graphs is what separates engineers who 'can code' from engineers who 'can solve problems'.
topics.analogy: A graph is like a flight network: cities are nodes, direct flights are edges. Finding the cheapest route from Delhi to New York (weighted shortest path) is Dijkstra. Checking if you can fly from any city to any other (connectivity) is Union-Find or BFS.
topics.memoryAnchor: BFS = ripples in water = shortest path (unweighted). DFS = going deep first = cycle detection, topological sort. Dijkstra = greedy BFS with priority queue = shortest path (weighted). Union-Find = merge circles of friends.
topics.firstPrinciples: Represent as adjacency list (Map<Integer, List<Integer>>) for sparse graphs, adjacency matrix (int[][]) for dense graphs. V = vertices, E = edges. BFS/DFS: O(V+E). Dijkstra: O((V+E) log V). Union-Find: O(α(V)) ≈ O(1). Always track visited to avoid infinite loops.
topics.bruteForce: For connectivity: try all paths between every pair — O(V * (V+E)). For shortest path: BFS from source, stop at target — already optimal for unweighted. For cycle detection: try all DFS paths — already O(V+E).
topics.optimizedApproach: Build adjacency list first. Always maintain a visited set/array. For grid problems, treat each cell as a node. For shortest path in unweighted graph → BFS. For weighted → Dijkstra. For cycle → DFS with grey/black coloring.
topics.whenToUse: Any problem involving nodes and connections: number of islands (grid graph), course schedule (directed graph + topological sort), network delay (weighted graph + Dijkstra), social network (undirected + BFS), word ladder (BFS on implicit graph).

examples.title: Number of Islands (BFS)
examples.explanation: Each '1' triggers a BFS that marks all connected land cells — one BFS per island. Count how many times BFS is triggered.

problems.title: Number of Islands
problems.description:
Given an m×n grid of '1' (land) and '0' (water), count the number of islands. An island is formed by connecting adjacent (horizontal/vertical) land cells.

**Example:**
Input:
11110
11010
11000
00000
Output: 1

Input:
11000
11000
00100
00011
Output: 3
problems.hint1: How do you mark land cells you've already counted so you don't count the same island twice?
problems.hint2: For each unvisited '1', start a BFS/DFS to mark all connected '1's as visited (or change to '0'). Each BFS/DFS call = one island.
problems.hint3: DFS: mark grid[r][c]='0', recurse on 4 neighbors if they're '1' and in bounds. Count how many times you initiate a DFS.
problems.constraints: m == grid.length | n == grid[i].length | 1 ≤ m, n ≤ 300 | grid[i][j] is '0' or '1'
problems.editorial: Grid DFS/BFS: O(m*n) time and space. Modifying the grid in-place avoids extra visited array. Each cell is processed at most once. BFS alternative: use a queue, same complexity.

problems.title: Clone Graph
problems.description:
Given a reference to a node in a connected undirected graph, return a deep copy of the graph.

Each node has a value (int) and a list of neighbors (List<Node>).

**Example:**
Input: adjList = [[2,4],[1,3],[2,4],[1,3]]
Output: Deep copy of the same structure
problems.hint1: How do you avoid infinite loops when cloning a graph with cycles?
problems.hint2: Use a HashMap from original node → cloned node. Before recursing, check if the node is already cloned.
problems.hint3: DFS: if node in map → return map.get(node). Else create clone, add to map, then for each neighbor recurse and add to clone's neighbors.
problems.constraints: 0 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 100 | 1 ≤ Node.val ≤ 100 | No repeated edges, no self-loops | Graph is connected
problems.editorial: HashMap acts as both visited set and clone registry. The key insight: add node to map BEFORE recursing into neighbors to handle cycles. O(V+E) time and space.

problems.title: Course Schedule (Cycle Detection)
problems.description:
There are numCourses courses labeled 0 to n-1. You're given prerequisites[i] = [ai, bi] meaning you must take course bi before ai. Return true if you can finish all courses.

**Example:**
numCourses=2, prerequisites=[[1,0]] → true (take 0 then 1)
numCourses=2, prerequisites=[[1,0],[0,1]] → false (cycle: 0 requires 1, 1 requires 0)
problems.hint1: This is a cycle detection problem in a directed graph. If there's a cycle, you can't finish all courses.
problems.hint2: Use Kahn's algorithm (BFS topological sort): compute in-degrees, process nodes with in-degree 0, if all nodes are processed → no cycle.
problems.hint3: Kahn's: if result.size() == numCourses after BFS → no cycle, return true. Otherwise a cycle exists.
problems.editorial: Kahn's BFS topological sort detects cycles naturally: if any node is never added to the queue (always has in-degree > 0 due to cycle), processed < numCourses. O(V+E) time and space.

problems.title: Network Delay Time (Dijkstra)
problems.description:
There are n network nodes labeled 1 to n. Times[i] = [ui, vi, wi] represents a directed edge from ui to vi with weight wi. Given starting node k, find how long it takes for all nodes to receive the signal. Return -1 if not all nodes can be reached.

**Example:**
times = [[2,1,1],[2,3,1],[3,4,1]], n=4, k=2 → Output: 2
problems.hint1: This is single-source shortest path from node k. Which algorithm handles weighted directed graphs?
problems.hint2: Dijkstra: use a min-heap of [distance, node]. Initialize dist[k]=0, all others infinity. Answer = max(dist) if all reachable.
problems.hint3: After Dijkstra, find max(dist). If any dist == infinity → return -1. Otherwise return the max (slowest node to receive).
problems.editorial: Dijkstra from source k. Stale entry check: if d > dist[u], skip. After completing, max of dist[] is the answer (all nodes receive signal when the last one does). O((V+E) log V) time.

problems.title: Number of Connected Components
problems.description:
Given n nodes (0 to n-1) and an array of undirected edges, find the number of connected components in the graph.

**Example:**
n=5, edges=[[0,1],[1,2],[3,4]] → Output: 2 (components: {0,1,2} and {3,4})
problems.hint1: A connected component is a group of nodes where any two nodes can reach each other. How do you count these groups?
problems.hint2: Union-Find: start with n components. For each edge, union the two nodes. If they were in different components, decrement the count.
problems.hint3: Initialize parent[i]=i, components=n. For each edge [u,v]: if find(u) != find(v), union them and components--. Return components.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 2000 | 1 ≤ edges.length ≤ 5000 | 0 ≤ ui, vi < n | No repeated edges, no self-loops
problems.editorial: Union-Find with path compression and union by rank. Each edge either merges two components (decrement count) or connects nodes in the same component (noop). O(E * α(n)) ≈ O(E) total. Alternative BFS/DFS: O(V+E) but requires adjacency list.

problems.title: Word Ladder
problems.description:
Given beginWord, endWord, and a wordList, find the length of the shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord. Each step changes exactly one letter and each intermediate word must be in wordList.

**Example:**
beginWord='hit', endWord='cog', wordList=['hot','dot','dog','lot','log','cog']
Output: 5 → hit→hot→dot→dog→cog
problems.hint1: This is a shortest path problem in an implicit graph. Nodes are words, edges connect words that differ by one letter.
problems.hint2: BFS from beginWord. At each level, find all words in wordList that differ by exactly one letter from the current word. Remove them from the wordList (to avoid revisiting).
problems.hint3: To find all words differing by one letter: either compare each candidate with current word (O(26*L) per word), or generate all L*26 variants and check if they're in wordSet.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ beginWord.length ≤ 10 | endWord.length == beginWord.length | 1 ≤ wordList.length ≤ 5000 | All words same length, lowercase letters
problems.editorial: BFS on implicit graph: generate all 26*L word variants per word, check against wordSet. Remove from wordSet on first visit to avoid revisiting. Level-by-level BFS gives shortest path. O(26 * L * N) time where N = wordList size and L = word length.
